title: Iran conflict: Pharma supply chains may take months to recover
summary: The recent ceasefire in the Iran conflict is a much-needed breath of fresh air for India's pharmaceutical sector, potentially rejuvenating exports. Nevertheless, industry specialists warn that it may take considerable time for supply chains to fully recover....
thbAction: Use the article as an entry point to pitch THB's commercial visibility layer for territory planning, account prioritization, and next-best-action for pharma leadership teams.
contentHtml:
New Delhi: Pharmaceutical industry experts said that a sustained ceasefire in the Iran conflict may not lead to immediate improvements in exports and drug prices.

They said supply chains disrupted by the conflict will take months to stabilise, and prices of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs)-the raw materials that go into medicines-are expected to soften only gradually.

The pace of recovery hinges on how quickly trade corridors reopen and inventory buffers are rebuilt, they said.

Also Read:'Israel wants to start peace talks with Lebanon'

"It will help stabilise but uncertainty remains. Inputs have already been bought by firms at higher prices. But overall it's a reprieve," said Ashok Kumar Madan, executive director of Indian Drug Manufacturers Association. With tensions between Iran and the US appearing to ease, a rebound in exports is widely anticipated. But industry experts are tempering expectations. "It will take at least a month or two to bring raw material prices back to normal," said Namit Joshi, chairman of Pharmaceuticals Export Promotion Council of India (Pharmexcil). He also called on the industry to treat the crisis as a lesson in building supply chain resilience, not just a problem to be managed in the moment.

For India, the stakes are significant. West Asia accounts for 5-6% of the country's total pharmaceutical exports, with the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Kuwait and Yemen among its most important markets, which are heavily dependent on Indian generics. The conflict has disrupted shipping routes, resulted in a surge in freight cost and squeezed API imports. Dinesh Dua, former chairman of Pharmexcil, said this could translate into a loss ranging from ₹2,500 crore to ₹5,000 crore.

Dua noted that demand for Indian pharma products is likely to pick up as the situation stabilises but flagged that the road to recovery will not be linear. "With tensions easing, India's pharma exports are expected to rebound, driven by growing demand for affordable generic medicines in West Asia," he said.

Industry people are adding a word of caution. "The situation remains highly volatile and unpredictable," said Rajiv Nath of AiMeD. "Our advisory is to wait and watch. Each day brings developments that could be either comforting or discomforting," he said.
